title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a comprehensive summary of the text in English, within the 10,000-character limit:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) and the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System held a joint meeting on September 19-20, 2023. The meeting was attended by Jerome Powell, Chair John C. Williams, and other committee members. No specific decisions or announcements were made during the meeting.

The FOMC reviewed developments in financial markets over the intermeeting period. US data releases showed greater economic resilience than previously thought, with nominal yields on longer-run Treasury securities increasing due to stronger economic data, potential increase in neutral policy rate, and greater economic uncertainty. Real yields exceeded nominal yields, implying a small decline in inflation compensation.

Inflation expectations remain well-anchored, while household and corporate borrowing rates increased, but may take time for past monetary policy actions to fully pass through to these sectors. The Federal Reserve has seen an increase in various financial conditions indexes due to rising Treasury yields and a stronger dollar.

Labor market imbalances are easing, with slower job growth and rising unemployment rate. Wage data shows slowing wage growth, with lower year-over-year changes in average hourly earnings and employment cost index. Consumer price inflation is slowing, with total PCE increasing 3.3% over the past year and core PCE at 4.2%.

Real GDP is expanding solidly in the third quarter, driven by private domestic final purchases. Foreign economic growth slowed in Q2 due to property sector troubles in China and monetary policy restraint in Europe.

Central banks in advanced economies raised interest rates to combat high core inflation, while those in emerging markets mostly held or cut rates. The US economy showed robust growth, leading to moderate increases in market-implied federal funds rate and Treasury yields. Long-term bond yields rose globally, driven by higher US yields and concerns about foreign economic outlook.

Credit card and auto loan interest rates increased in July and August. Bank credit conditions tightened slightly, but access to credit remained generally accessible for businesses and households. Commercial real estate (CRE) loans increased, while nonfarm nonresidential loans contracted for the first time since March 2022.

The Federal Reserve maintained its target federal funds rate at 5-1/4 to 5-1/2 percent. Participants noted that economic activity was resilient, but inflation remained above the Committee's goal of 2%. Tight credit conditions and labor market imbalances were seen as headwinds. The majority judged one more rate increase likely, while some saw no further increases needed.

The Committee discussed monetary policy for this meeting. Members agreed that economic activity has been expanding at a solid pace, but job gains have slowed in recent months. Inflation remains elevated, and the unemployment rate is low. The US banking system is sound and resilient, but tighter credit conditions may weigh on economic activity and inflation.

Key statistics include:

* Unemployment rate: 3.8% (August)
* Labor force participation rate: up slightly
* Employment cost index: lower year-over-year changes in average hourly earnings

The Federal Reserve will continue to assess additional information and its implications for monetary policy. The Committee will reduce its holdings of Treasury securities, agency debt, and mortgage-backed securities.

release_time: For release at 2:00 p.m. EDT Share
title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
body:
Recent indicators suggest that economic activity expanded at a strong pace in the third quarter. Job gains have moderated since earlier in the year but remain strong, and the unemployment rate has remained low. Inflation remains elevated.
The U.S. banking system is sound and resilient. Tighter financial and credit conditions for households and businesses are likely to weigh on economic activity, hiring, and inflation. The extent of these effects remains uncertain. The Committee remains highly attentive to inflation risks.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. In support of these goals, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 5-1/4 to 5-1/2 percent. The Committee will continue to assess additional information and its implications for monetary policy. In determining the extent of additional policy firming that may be appropriate to return inflation to 2 percent over time, the Committee will take into account the cumulative tightening of monetary policy, the lags with which monetary policy affects economic activity and inflation, and economic and financial developments. In addition, the Committee will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities, as described in its previously announced plans. The Committee is strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Michael S. Barr; Michelle W. Bowman; Lisa D. Cook; Austan D. Goolsbee; Patrick Harker; Philip N. Jefferson; Neel Kashkari; Adriana D. Kugler; Lorie K. Logan; and Christopher J. Waller.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the text in English, under 10,000 characters:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) and the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System held a joint meeting to discuss key issues affecting the economy. The main topics included:

* Developments in financial markets: Tightening conditions were driven by higher Treasury yields, lower equity prices, and a stronger dollar.
* Labor market conditions: Job gains continued to be strong, with an unchanged unemployment rate of 3.8%.
* Consumer price inflation: Total PCE price inflation increased, but showed signs of slowing down.
* Real GDP growth: Strong gain in the third quarter driven by private domestic final purchases.

The FOMC maintained its target range for the federal funds rate at 5-1/4 to 5-1/2 percent, aiming to achieve maximum employment and inflation at a rate of 2 percent over the longer run. The Committee will continue to assess additional information and implications for monetary policy to achieve these goals.

Key points:

* Interest rates increased for various loans, including residential mortgages, auto loans, and credit cards.
* Credit remained generally available to businesses, households, and municipalities.
* Small firms found it harder to obtain credit, with more difficulty reported compared to three months earlier.

Important numbers:

* Unemployment rate: 3.8%
* Inflation: 2%

Overall, the Federal Reserve is committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective and will monitor incoming information for any risks that could impede this goal.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a comprehensive summary of the text in English, within the 799-word limit:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held a joint meeting with the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System on December 12-13, 2023. The committee reviewed developments in financial markets and open market operations over the intermeeting period.

Key findings included:

* Financial conditions eased due to declining interest rates, rising equity prices, and a depreciating dollar.
* Equity prices rose, supported by declining Treasury yields and strong earnings growth.
* Implied volatility for equities decreased significantly.

The committee's key conclusions were:

1. Yields on nominal Treasury securities declined sharply over the period, with longer-term maturities experiencing more significant declines.
2. Market participants interpreted FOMC communications as suggesting that the policy rate may be near its peak.
3. Treasury yields fell, and credit spreads narrowed, leading to a decline in borrowing costs for businesses, households, and municipalities.

The labor market expanded at a slower pace in October-November compared to Q3. The unemployment rate remained steady at 3.7% in November, while the labor force participation rate was flat. Job openings continued to trend down, and quits were unchanged.

Inflation remained elevated but showed signs of easing. Total PCE increased 3% over the past 12 months, while core PCE was up 3.5%. Both measures were lower than a year ago. Inflation expectations for medium- to longer-term periods declined.

The economic forecast for December remained similar to previous projections. GDP growth is expected to slow in Q4 but remain solid overall. Inflation forecast was revised down due to lower-than-expected data and judged to be less persistent.

Participants' projections showed a range of views on GDP growth, unemployment, and inflation from 2023 to 2026. The Federal Reserve's Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) suggests that economic growth will cool down in 2024, with job gains moderating and the unemployment rate rising slightly.

The Committee maintained its restrictive policy stance, citing progress toward inflation target (2%) and labor market balance. Most participants project a lower target range for federal funds rate by end of 2024, reflecting improved inflation outlooks. However, they note high uncertainty and possibility of upside risks to inflation or need for longer restrictive stance.

The FOMC voted to maintain the federal funds rate target range at 5-1/4 to 5-1/2 percent. The Committee plans to slow and stop balance sheet reduction when reserve balances are adequate. Members agreed that recent indicators suggest slowing economic growth, but job gains remain strong and unemployment rate is low.

The Federal Reserve will:

* Redeem Treasury securities up to $20 billion per month
* Reinvest agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) exceeding $35 billion per month
* Engage in dollar roll and coupon swap transactions for operational reasons

The Fed's goal is maximum employment and inflation at 2% over the longer run. The current economic indicators show slower growth, but job gains remain strong and unemployment remains low. Inflation has eased, but remains elevated; tighter financial conditions may weigh on economic activity.

**Key Statistics:**

* Inflation has continued to ease
* GDP growth is expected to slow in Q4 but remain solid overall
* Unemployment rate remained steady at 3.7% in November

**Important Numbers:**

* $1.3 trillion: Reduction in take-up at the ON RRP facility since early June
* 25 basis points: expected decline in federal funds rate by May 2024
* 45 basis points: decline in overnight reverse repurchase agreement (ON RRP) rates

Overall, the Federal Reserve's monetary policy decisions aim to achieve maximum employment and inflation at 2% over the longer run. The Committee will continue to assess data and adjust policy as needed to ensure a strong economy with sustainable growth and low inflation.

release_time: For release at 2:00 p.m. EST Share
title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
body:
Indicators of economic activity and employment have continued to strengthen. The sectors most adversely affected by the pandemic have improved in recent months but are being affected by the recent sharp rise in COVID-19 cases. Job gains have been solid in recent months, and the unemployment rate has declined substantially. Supply and demand imbalances related to the pandemic and the reopening of the economy have continued to contribute to elevated levels of inflation. Overall financial conditions remain accommodative, in part reflecting policy measures to support the economy and the flow of credit to U.S. households and businesses.
The path of the economy continues to depend on the course of the virus. Progress on vaccinations and an easing of supply constraints are expected to support continued gains in economic activity and employment as well as a reduction in inflation. Risks to the economic outlook remain, including from new variants of the virus.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. In support of these goals, the Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent. With inflation well above 2 percent and a strong labor market, the Committee expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range for the federal funds rate. The Committee decided to continue to reduce the monthly pace of its net asset purchases, bringing them to an end in early March. Beginning in February, the Committee will increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $20 billion per month and of agency mortgageâbacked securities by at least $10 billion per month. The Federal Reserve's ongoing purchases and holdings of securities will continue to foster smooth market functioning and accommodative financial conditions, thereby supporting the flow of credit to households and businesses.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Michelle W. Bowman; Lael Brainard; James Bullard; Esther L. George; Patrick Harker; Loretta J. Mester; and Christopher J. Waller. Patrick Harker voted as an alternate member at this meeting.

title: Implementation Note issued January 26, 2022
body:

The Federal Reserve has made the following decisions to implement the monetary policy stance announced by the Federal Open Market Committee in its statement on January 26, 2022:
- The Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System voted unanimously to maintain the interest rate paid on reserve balances at 0.15 percent, effective January 27, 2022.

- As part of its policy decision, the Federal Open Market Committee voted to authorize and direct the Open Market Desk at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, until instructed otherwise, to execute transactions in the System Open Market Account in accordance with the following domestic policy directive: "Effective January 27, 2022, the Federal Open Market Committee directs the Desk to: Undertake open market operations as necessary to maintain the federal funds rate in a target range of 0 to 1/4 percent. Complete the increase in System Open Market Account (SOMA) holdings of Treasury securities by $40 billion and of agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) by $20 billion, as indicated in the monthly purchase plans released in mid-January. Increase the SOMA holdings of Treasury securities by $20 billion and of agency MBS by $10 billion, during the monthly purchase period beginning in mid-February. Increase holdings of Treasury securities and agency MBS by additional amounts as needed to sustain smooth functioning of markets for these securities. Conduct overnight repurchase agreement operations with a minimum bid rate of 0.25 percent and with an aggregate operation limit of $500 billion; the aggregate operation limit can be temporarily increased at the discretion of the Chair. Conduct overnight reverse repurchase agreement operations at an offering rate of 0.05 percent and with a per-counterparty limit of $160 billion per day; the per-counterparty limit can be temporarily increased at the discretion of the Chair. Roll over at auction all principal payments from the Federal Reserve's holdings of Treasury securities and reinvest all principal payments from the Federal Reserve's holdings of agency debt and agency MBS in agency MBS. Allow modest deviations from stated amounts for purchases and reinvestments, if needed for operational reasons. Engage in dollar roll and coupon swap transactions as necessary to facilitate settlement of the Federal Reserve's agency MBS transactions."

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the text in English, under 10,000 characters:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held a joint meeting with the Board of Governors on January 25-26, 2022. The meeting was attended by Fed officials, including Chair Jerome Powell and Vice Chair John Williams.

Key points:

* The FOMC reaffirmed its authorization for domestic open market operations to implement policy directives.
* The Committee authorized foreign currency operations, including purchasing and selling obligations of foreign governments or agencies.
* The Federal Reserve set rules for managing foreign currency liquidity swap arrangements.
* The Committee discussed reducing the size of the Federal Reserve's balance sheet.
* Participants expected inflation rates to decrease as supply constraints ease, but with some uncertainty.

Conclusion:

The FOMC meeting highlighted the Committee's commitment to promoting maximum employment and price stability. Despite recent COVID-19 cases, job gains have been solid, and unemployment rates have declined. Inflation remains elevated due to pandemic-related supply and demand imbalances. The Committee expects inflation to soon be above target, warranting a tightening of monetary policy.

Important numbers:

* 0-1/4%: Federal funds rate target range
* $40 billion: Increase in Treasury securities holdings
* $20 billion: Increase in agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) holdings
* 2.6%: PCE price inflation rate for 2022
* 2%: PCE price inflation rate for 2023 and 2024

Document's purpose:

The document outlines the Federal Reserve's guidelines for managing foreign currencies, open market operations, and balance sheet runoff.

Scope:

The document covers various aspects of monetary policy implementation, including domestic and foreign currency operations, balance sheet management, and interest rates.

release_time: For release at 2:00 p.m. EDT Share
title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
body:
Indicators of economic activity and employment have continued to strengthen. Job gains have been strong in recent months, and the unemployment rate has declined substantially. Inflation remains elevated, reflecting supply and demand imbalances related to the pandemic, higher energy prices, and broader price pressures.
The invasion of Ukraine by Russia is causing tremendous human and economic hardship. The implications for the U.S. economy are highly uncertain, but in the near term the invasion and related events are likely to create additional upward pressure on inflation and weigh on economic activity.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. With appropriate firming in the stance of monetary policy, the Committee expects inflation to return to its 2 percent objective and the labor market to remain strong. In support of these goals, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/4 to 1/2 percent and anticipates that ongoing increases in the target range will be appropriate. In addition, the Committee expects to begin reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities at a coming meeting.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Michelle W. Bowman; Lael Brainard; Esther L. George; Patrick Harker; Loretta J. Mester; and Christopher J. Waller. Voting against this action was James Bullard, who preferred at this meeting to raise the target range for the federal funds rate by 0.5 percentage point to 1/2 to 3/4 percent. Patrick Harker voted as an alternate member at this meeting.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the text in English, within the 10,000-character limit:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held a joint meeting with the Board of Governors on March 15-16, 2022. The key takeaways are as follows:

* The FOMC aims to slow down and eventually stop balance sheet runoff as the size of its System Open Market Account (SOMA) portfolio approaches the Committee's ample-reserves framework.
* Participants agree to start balance sheet runoff at the next meeting, with a faster pace than previous years (2017-19).
* The runoff will be done by adjusting reinvestments of principal payments received from securities held in the SOMA.

The economic outlook is strong, with private-sector job openings rates above pre-pandemic levels. Average hourly earnings rose 5.1% over the past year. Consumer prices continued to rise rapidly, with total PCE price inflation at 6.1%. Core CPI inflation was 5.2%, and trimmed mean measure of 12-month PCE inflation was 3.5%.

Inflation compensation measures increased sharply across advanced economies. Emerging market currencies depreciated outside of Eastern Europe, with sovereign spreads widening.

The Federal Reserve will continue to hold agency MBS for many years after balance sheet runoff. The Committee may consider selling agency MBS to compose primarily of Treasury securities.

The US labor market remains strong, with private-sector job openings rates above pre-pandemic levels. Average hourly earnings rose 5.1% over the past year. Consumer prices continued to rise rapidly, with total PCE price inflation at 6.1%.

International trade fell due to reduced travel and services trade remained low. Omicron variant tempered foreign recovery, while COVID-19 cases decreased, easing restrictions.

Russian invasion of Ukraine caused commodity price increases, hurt global risk sentiment, and exacerbated supply bottlenecks. Inflation abroad rose driven by recovering demand, rising prices, and supply chain strains.

The intermeeting period saw highly volatile financial markets due to geopolitical developments, central bank communications, and rising inflation concerns. Sovereign yields increased, despite downward pressure from Russia's invasion of Ukraine, driven by higher-than-expected inflation readings and less accommodative central bank communications.

The Federal Reserve aims to bring inflation back to its 2% target over time. The invasion of Ukraine is causing economic hardship and uncertainty, leading to increased inflation pressure. Real GDP growth has slowed due to weaker inventory investment, but consumption and business investment remain strong.

Labor market fundamentals are solid, with high demand for labor exceeding supply. Nominal wages are rising rapidly across all income levels, driven by a tight labor market. Participants expect the labor market to remain tight.

The Federal Reserve Committee emphasized its strong commitment to restore price stability. Members agreed that monetary policy should move toward a neutral posture expeditiously and be prepared to adjust in response to evolving economic conditions.

Elevated inflation and inflation expectations pose significant risks, particularly if the public questions the Committee's resolve. The committee anticipates ongoing increases in the target range and is prepared to adjust policy if risks emerge.

In conclusion, the Federal Reserve aims to achieve maximum employment and price stability over time. The Committee raised the federal funds rate target range to 1/4 to 1/2 percent to achieve this goal.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a comprehensive summary of the text in English, within the 10,000-word limit:

**Summary:**

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held several meetings in 2022 to discuss monetary policy and economic developments. The committee reviewed economic data, discussed inflation expectations, and considered potential risks to the economy.

**Key Points:**

1. The FOMC maintained its federal funds rate target range of 3/4% to 1% and planned to slow down and eventually stop reducing its balance sheet size.
2. The committee anticipated ongoing increases in the target range for monetary policy to achieve maximum employment and a 2% inflation rate over the long run.
3. Economic activity edged down in Q1, but household spending and business investment remained strong, with job gains robust and unemployment rates declining.
4. Inflation remains elevated due to pandemic-related supply chain disruptions and higher energy prices, while Russia's invasion of Ukraine may exacerbate inflation and economic uncertainty.
5. The Fed seeks to restore price stability and ensure maximum employment.

**Notable Numbers:**

1. Dollar appreciated by 2% against broad trade-weighted dollar.
2. Yen depreciated 9% against the dollar.
3. Unemployment rate declined to 3.6%.
4. Total PCE price inflation remained elevated at 6.6% over 12 months.

**Key Conclusions:**

1. The Federal Reserve's Committee aims to keep inflation low and unemployment strong by raising the federal funds rate target range and reducing its Treasury securities and agency debt holdings.
2. The committee will monitor economic data and adjust policy as needed to achieve its goals, considering various factors including public health, labor market conditions, inflation expectations, and financial developments.

**Important Quotes:**

1. "The Committee reaffirms its commitment to restoring price stability over the long run."
2. "The FOMC decision is aimed at maintaining maximum employment while achieving a 2% inflation rate."

Overall, the Federal Reserve's meetings in 2022 focused on addressing concerns about inflation, supply chain disruptions, and potential economic risks related to global events such as Russia's invasion of Ukraine. The committee maintained its federal funds rate target range and planned to slow down and eventually stop reducing its balance sheet size.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a comprehensive summary of the text in English, within the 10,000-character limit:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held a joint meeting with the Board of Governors to discuss monetary policy. The key takeaway was that no specific conclusions or decisions were announced.

Notable statistics include a decrease in Treasury yields, reflecting expectations of slower growth and lower inflation compensation. Market participants expected significant policy tightening in coming meetings to respond to current elevated levels of inflation.

The FOMC meeting concluded with a neutral policy rate hike, raising the target federal funds rate range to 2-1/4 to 2-1/2 percent. Participants agreed that ongoing increases in the federal funds target range are necessary to achieve the committee's objectives, given inflation above its 2% objective.

The global economy faced headwinds from energy disruptions, political uncertainty in Europe, and tighter monetary conditions. Commodity prices fell, but foreign consumer inflation remained high due to past increases in energy and food prices.

US real GDP declined in the first half, but labor market remains tight with strong demand. Consumer price inflation remained elevated at 6.3% over 12 months ending May (core PCE: 4.7%). Nominal wage growth was rapid and broad-based, up 5.1% over 12 months ending June.

The Federal Reserve emphasized that slowing aggregate demand would help reduce inflation pressures. Participants agreed that firming monetary policy, anchored longer-term inflation expectations, and competitive pressures would bring inflation back down to 2%.

Financial stability has eased due to strong capital and liquidity levels, healthy household balance sheets, and improved mortgage underwriting standards. However, some participants warned against complacency, emphasizing the need to consider various economic scenarios.

The FOMC will continue to raise interest rates to combat high inflation. The pace of rate hikes will depend on incoming information. The Committee aims to return inflation to its 2% objective and noted that job gains have been robust, but spending and production indicators have softened.

In conclusion, the FOMC meeting focused on monetary policy, with no specific conclusions or decisions announced. The global economy faced headwinds, and the US labor market remains tight despite declining GDP. Inflation remained elevated, and financial stability has eased, but participants emphasized the need to consider various economic scenarios.

Important numbers include:

* Federal funds rate: 2.25-2.5%
* Minimum bid rate for overnight repurchase agreements: 2.5%
* Aggregate operation limit: $500 billion
* Per-counterparty limit for overnight reverse repurchase agreements: $160 billion per day

Overall, the FOMC meeting highlighted the need to continue raising interest rates to combat high inflation and maintain financial stability.

title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
body:
Recent indicators point to modest growth in spending and production. Job gains have been robust in recent months, and the unemployment rate has remained low. Inflation remains elevated, reflecting supply and demand imbalances related to the pandemic, higher food and energy prices, and broader price pressures.
Russia's war against Ukraine is causing tremendous human and economic hardship. The war and related events are creating additional upward pressure on inflation and are weighing on global economic activity. The Committee is highly attentive to inflation risks.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. In support of these goals, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 3 to 3-1/4 percent and anticipates that ongoing increases in the target range will be appropriate. In addition, the Committee will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities, as described in the Plans for Reducing the Size of the Federal Reserve's Balance Sheet that were issued in May. The Committee is strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Michael S. Barr; Michelle W. Bowman; Lael Brainard; James Bullard; Susan M. Collins; Lisa D. Cook; Esther L. George; Philip N. Jefferson; Loretta J. Mester; and Christopher J. Waller.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the text in English, within the 705-character limit:

**Summary**

The Federal Reserve's Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) met to discuss monetary policy. The committee noted that real GDP was growing modestly after declining earlier in the year. Labor demand remained strong, with robust job gains in July and August. Consumer price inflation remained elevated at 6.3% over 12 months.

The FOMC raised the federal funds target range by 75 basis points to 3-3.25%. The committee projected moderate economic growth in H2 of this year, but below-trend pace for the next few years. The FOMC aims to achieve maximum employment and price stability.

**Key Points**

* Real GDP growing modestly
* Labor demand strong, job gains robust
* Consumer price inflation elevated at 6.3%
* Federal funds target range raised by 75 basis points to 3-3.25%

**Notable Numbers**

* Nominal U.S. international trade deficit continues to narrow
* Net exports contribute positively to GDP growth in Q2

This summary aims to capture the main points and key statistics from the text, while staying within the 705-character limit.

body:

The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the text in English, within the 666-character limit:

The Federal Reserve's November meeting discussed market conditions and economic situation. The Committee ratified the Desk's domestic transactions and did not intervene in foreign currencies. Key takeaways: financial markets remain volatile, US economic growth forecast weakened due to restrictive financial conditions and monetary policy assumptions. Inflation expected to decline gradually, but risks remain skewed to the upside.

Key points:

* The Committee discussed slowing down future rate hikes due to tightening financial conditions.
* Most respondents expected a 50 basis point increase in the federal funds rate at the December meeting.
* Treasury yields and investment-grade bond yields rose due to upward revisions in policy rates and term premiums.

Conclusions:

* The Federal Reserve will likely maintain a restrictive monetary policy stance in light of higher-than-expected core inflation.
* Market participants are expecting a 75 basis point increase in the federal funds rate at the next meeting.

Important numbers:

* Total PCE price inflation: 5.3% in 2022, 2% in 2025
* Core PCE price inflation: 4.6% in 2022, 2% in 2025

Global financial markets experienced volatility due to global growth concerns and synchronous policy tightening by major central banks. The US dollar appreciated against most currencies, while the Japanese yen weakened and the Chinese renminbi depreciated significantly.

The Committee emphasized the need for restrictive monetary policy to achieve its dual-mandate goals of maximum employment and inflation at 2%. Members will consider cumulative tightening, lags, and economic/financial developments when setting future interest rates. The FOMC will continue reducing the Fed's balance sheet by selling Treasury securities, agency debt, and agency MBS.

Overall, the Federal Reserve aims to achieve a sufficiently restrictive stance of monetary policy to return inflation to 2%.

- Engage in dollar roll and coupon swap transactions as necessary to facilitate settlement of the Federal Reserve's agency MBS transactions."
- In a related action, the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System voted unanimously to approve a 1/2 percentage point increase in the primary credit rate to 4.5 percent, effective December 15, 2022. In taking this action, the Board approved requests to establish that rate submitted by the Boards of Directors of the Federal Reserve Banks of Boston, New York, Philadelphia, Cleveland, Richmond, Atlanta, Chicago, St. Louis, Minneapolis, Kansas City, Dallas, and San Francisco.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a comprehensive English summary of the text, within the 10,000 character limit:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held several meetings to discuss economic conditions, inflation expectations, and monetary policy. The committee's key goals are maximum employment and inflation at 2%. To achieve this, it raised the federal funds target range to 4-1/4 to 4-1/2 percent.

The FOMC noted that US GDP growth was modest, labor market conditions were easing, and consumer price inflation remained elevated (6.0%). Nominal wage growth averaged 5.1% over 12 months. Inflation expectations were steady, with the core PCE price index dropping to 5.0%.

Regarding monetary policy, the committee emphasized its commitment to returning inflation to its 2% objective. It raised interest rates to curb inflation and maintained flexibility in adjusting policy.

Financial markets have eased after several months of tightening, with investor concerns about global risks decreasing and inflationary pressures moderating. Implied volatility across financial markets declined from October levels.

Key points:

* US GDP growth was modest
* Labor market conditions eased
* Consumer price inflation remained elevated (6.0%)
* Nominal wage growth averaged 5.1% over 12 months
* Inflation expectations were steady
* Core PCE price index dropped to 5.0%
* FOMC raised federal funds target range to 4-1/4 to 4-1/2 percent

Overall, the FOMC's decisions aim to achieve maximum employment and inflation at 2%, while minimizing economic disruption.

release_time: For release at 2:00 p.m. EST Share
title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
body:
The Federal Reserve is committed to using its full range of tools to support the U.S. economy in this challenging time, thereby promoting its maximum employment and price stability goals.
The COVID-19 pandemic is causing tremendous human and economic hardship across the United States and around the world. The pace of the recovery in economic activity and employment has moderated in recent months, with weakness concentrated in the sectors most adversely affected by the pandemic. Weaker demand and earlier declines in oil prices have been holding down consumer price inflation. Overall financial conditions remain accommodative, in part reflecting policy measures to support the economy and the flow of credit to U.S. households and businesses.
The path of the economy will depend significantly on the course of the virus, including progress on vaccinations. The ongoing public health crisis continues to weigh on economic activity, employment, and inflation, and poses considerable risks to the economic outlook.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. With inflation running persistently below this longer-run goal, the Committee will aim to achieve inflation moderately above 2 percent for some time so that inflation averages 2 percent over time and longerâterm inflation expectations remain well anchored at 2 percent. The Committee expects to maintain an accommodative stance of monetary policy until these outcomes are achieved. The Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent and expects it will be appropriate to maintain this target range until labor market conditions have reached levels consistent with the Committee's assessments of maximum employment and inflation has risen to 2 percent and is on track to moderately exceed 2 percent for some time. In addition, the Federal Reserve will continue to increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $80 billion per month and of agency mortgageâbacked securities by at least $40 billion per month until substantial further progress has been made toward the Committee's maximum employment and price stability goals. These asset purchases help foster smooth market functioning and accommodative financial conditions, thereby supporting the flow of credit to households and businesses.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Thomas I. Barkin; Raphael W. Bostic; Michelle W. Bowman; Lael Brainard; Richard H. Clarida; Mary C. Daly; Charles L. Evans; Randal K. Quarles; and Christopher J. Waller.

body:

- As part of its policy decision, the Federal Open Market Committee voted to authorize and direct the Open Market Desk at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, until instructed otherwise, to execute transactions in the System Open Market Account in accordance with the following domestic policy directive: "Effective January 28, 2021, the Federal Open Market Committee directs the Desk to: Undertake open market operations as necessary to maintain the federal funds rate in a target range of 0 to 1/4 percent. Increase the System Open Market Account holdings of Treasury securities by $80 billion per month and of agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) by $40 billion per month. Conduct repurchase agreement operations to support effective policy implementation and the smooth functioning of short-term U.S. dollar funding markets. Allow modest deviations from stated amounts for purchases and reinvestments, if needed for operational reasons. body:

The Federal Reserve System held several meetings to discuss its monetary policy and operational framework. The Committee reaffirmed its authorizations for open market operations, foreign currency transactions, and system open market account management. It also authorized the Selected Bank to conduct reverse repo transactions, foreign currency liquidity swap arrangements, and warehousing transactions.

The Committee discussed the COVID-19 pandemic's impact on the economy, acknowledging significant risks to economic activity, employment, and inflation. It agreed to maintain an accommodative monetary policy stance until maximum employment and price stability (2% inflation) are achieved.

Key points:

* The Federal Reserve will continue buying Treasury securities and agency mortgage-backed securities to support the economy.
* The goal is to achieve maximum employment and price stability.
* The Fed will monitor economic data and adjust policy as needed to address risks.

Notable numbers:

* $80 billion/month: Treasury securities purchases
* $40 billion/month: Agency mortgage-backed securities purchases
* 2%: Target inflation rate

Summary:
The Federal Reserve aims to achieve maximum employment and price stability by using its full range of tools. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the economy has faced significant challenges, with moderate recovery pace and weaker demand. The Committee seeks to maintain an accommodative stance until its goals are achieved.

This summary includes key points, important numbers, and a concise summary of the text's main themes and takeaways.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a comprehensive summary of the text in English, within the 10000-character limit:

**Summary**

The Federal Reserve's Committee met to discuss monetary policy, reviewing its Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) rules and discussing financial markets. The balance sheet has expanded significantly over the past year, with reserve balances rising by over $400 billion to $3.7 trillion. The Overnight Reverse Repurchase Agreement (ON RRP) facility was reviewed to alleviate downward pressure on overnight rates.

**Key Points**

* Real GDP expanded faster in Q1 2021 than Q4 2020, but still below pre-pandemic levels.
* Labor market conditions improved in January and February, with employment increasing solidly over these months.
* Inflation remains stable at 1.5% over the past 12 months, with core PCE price inflation also at 1.5%.
* The federal funds rate target range remains at 0-1/4%, and asset purchases will continue until substantial progress is made toward maximum employment and price stability goals.
* The Committee aims to achieve moderate inflation above 2% to average 2% over time.

**Important Numbers**

* $400 billion: Increase in reserve balances over the past year.
* $3.7 trillion: Current level of reserve balances.
* 30 days: Time frame for revised FOIA rules to take effect.
* $80 billion: Per-counterparty limit for ON RRP facility.
* 2%: Inflation goal.

**Conclusion**

The Federal Reserve aims to achieve maximum employment and inflation at 2% over the long term, with a focus on labor market conditions and inflation reaching 2%. The Committee will maintain an accommodative monetary policy stance until these outcomes are achieved.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a final summary of the text in English, within the 10,000-character limit:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) and the Board of Governors held a joint meeting via videoconference on June 15-16, 2021. The main discussion focused on designing domestic and foreign international repurchase agreement facilities to foster effective monetary policy implementation and market smoothness.

Key points include:

* No specific monetary policy decisions were made during the meeting.
* The Federal Reserve System's Board of Governors and its divisions discussed repurchase agreement facilities to foster effective monetary policy implementation and market smoothness.
* Key considerations included minimum bid rate at 25 basis points (top of federal funds target range), counterparties, eligible collateral, and participant support for the terms.

The FOMC staff presented potential terms for a domestic Standing Repo Facility (SRF) with a minimum bid rate at 25 basis points, counterparties being primary dealers and banks that express interest, and eligible collateral including US Treasury securities or all securities accepted in open market operations. Participants broadly supported the terms, noting the importance of setting terms to promote equitable access to liquidity.

The FOMC discussed adjusting the Federal Reserve's (Fed) operations over time, including moving to a cleared settlement structure. Most participants supported allowing banks to be counterparties to a domestic Standing Repurchase Agreement Facility (SRF), which could address liquidity pressures in the federal funds market and promote equitable access to liquidity.

US financial conditions eased further, led by a decline in Treasury yields. Market participants focused on data showing lower employment growth and higher inflation readings. Median forecasts for PCE inflation rose nearly 1 percentage point from the previous survey.

The FOMC discussed overnight rates, administered rates, and emergency facilities. They considered raising the federal funds rate to support smooth functioning of short-term funding markets. The manager noted that balances at the ON RRP facility would likely continue to grow. The committee also discussed winding down emergency facilities established during the pandemic. Additionally, they proposed extending temporary U.S. dollar liquidity swap arrangements through December 31, 2021.

As of May, payroll employment had recovered two-thirds of job losses from pandemic onset. Unemployment rate edged down to 5.8% in May, with African Americans, Asians, and Hispanics seeing slight declines. Labor force participation rate and employment-to-population ratio rose slightly. Initial unemployment claims decreased but remained high relative to pre-pandemic levels. Private-sector payrolls showed increased gains late in May.

The economy showed signs of strengthening in April, particularly in exports of capital goods. Global semiconductor shortages continued to impact automotive product exports and imports. Services sector exports remained depressed due to limited international travel.

Corporate bond spreads narrowed slightly for all credit ratings. Yields on municipal bonds reached historical lows, with spreads unchanged or narrower for higher-credit-rated bonds. Short-term funding markets functioned smoothly, with short-term rates near historical lows. Government money market funds attracted inflows and invested in Overnight Reverse Repo (ON RRP) to earn nonnegative yields.

Municipal bond issuance and credit quality improved slightly. Small business loan originations rose, but demand remained subdued outside of PPP. Commercial real estate financing conditions were mostly accommodative. Residential mortgage market remained accommodative for stronger borrowers. Consumer credit grew at a robust pace in April.

Unemployment rate dropped to historically low levels, while inflation rose due to base effects and supply constraints. Inflation expected to slow down gradually as base effects fade and demand growth eases. Transitory price increases from bottlenecks and supply constraints expected to reverse by 2022. Inflation forecast to be slightly above 2% in 2023, supported by high resource utilization.

The FOMC concluded that the economy will likely converge under appropriate monetary policy and no further shocks. Progress on vaccinations has reduced COVID-19 spread, leading to strengthening economic indicators and employment. Inflation rose due to transitory factors. The path of the economy depends on the virus's course. Economic activity is expanding rapidly, driven by consumer spending.

Labor market conditions have improved, with job gains and falling unemployment rate. Labor shortages are putting upward pressure on wages and prices. Inflation has risen above expectations due to supply chain constraints and rebounding demand. Participants expect inflation to ease as transitory factors dissipate, but anticipate ongoing pressures from supply chain limitations.

The FOMC assessed the sustainability of inflation pressures, noting stable readings around 2% and consistent longer-term expectations. Financial conditions remained accommodative, but low interest rates fueled elevated house prices, posing stability risks. Participants highlighted supply chain disruptions and labor shortages, judging uncertainty around economic projections was elevated.

Let me know if you'd like me to expand on this summary or clarify any points!

title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
body:
The Federal Reserve is committed to using its full range of tools to support the U.S. economy in this challenging time, thereby promoting its maximum employment and price stability goals.
With progress on vaccinations and strong policy support, indicators of economic activity and employment have continued to strengthen. The sectors most adversely affected by the pandemic have shown improvement but have not fully recovered. Inflation has risen, largely reflecting transitory factors. Overall financial conditions remain accommodative, in part reflecting policy measures to support the economy and the flow of credit to U.S. households and businesses.
The path of the economy continues to depend on the course of the virus. Progress on vaccinations will likely continue to reduce the effects of the public health crisis on the economy, but risks to the economic outlook remain.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. With inflation having run persistently below this longer-run goal, the Committee will aim to achieve inflation moderately above 2 percent for some time so that inflation averages 2 percent over time and longerâterm inflation expectations remain well anchored at 2 percent. The Committee expects to maintain an accommodative stance of monetary policy until these outcomes are achieved. The Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent and expects it will be appropriate to maintain this target range until labor market conditions have reached levels consistent with the Committee's assessments of maximum employment and inflation has risen to 2 percent and is on track to moderately exceed 2 percent for some time. Last December, the Committee indicated that it would continue to increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $80 billion per month and of agency mortgageâbacked securities by at least $40 billion per month until substantial further progress has been made toward its maximum employment and price stability goals. Since then, the economy has made progress toward these goals, and the Committee will continue to assess progress in coming meetings. These asset purchases help foster smooth market functioning and accommodative financial conditions, thereby supporting the flow of credit to households and businesses.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Thomas I. Barkin; Raphael W. Bostic; Michelle W. Bowman; Lael Brainard; Richard H. Clarida; Mary C. Daly; Charles L. Evans; Randal K. Quarles; and Christopher J. Waller.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the text in English, within the 10000-character limit:

The Federal Reserve's Open Market Operations Committee discussed economic conditions and monetary policy options. Despite some notable market moves, overall financial conditions remained historically accommodative. Key takeaways include:

* The economy has continued to strengthen due to progress on vaccinations and policy support.
* Labor force participation and employment-to-population ratios have shown little change.
* Unemployment rate remains high at 5.9% in June.
* Inflation has risen due to transitory factors, and overall financial conditions remain accommodative.

The Committee discussed the economic outlook, highlighting uncertainty and risks. Some participants noted upside risks to inflation due to supply chain disruptions and labor shortages. Others emphasized financial stability risks from elevated asset valuations and housing market increases.

The Committee reaffirmed its commitment to using all tools to support the economy and promote maximum employment and price stability. The target federal funds rate remains at 0-1/4%, and asset purchases continue until substantial progress towards goals is achieved.

Overall, the Federal Reserve maintained an accommodative monetary policy stance to support the US economy, with a focus on achieving maximum employment and price stability.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the text in English, within the 10000-character limit:

**Federal Reserve Meeting Summary**

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) met to discuss monetary policy and economic conditions. The meeting was held by videoconference on September 21-22, 2021.

**Key Points:**

* The economy showed resilience in the third quarter, with real PCE declining only slightly.
* Retail sales rebounded in August, but consumer services spending was affected by pandemic-related concerns.
* Housing demand remained strong, but construction activity was restrained by materials shortages.
* Business fixed investment growth slowed due to supply bottlenecks.

**Economic Outlook:**

* The staff's economic outlook suggests that inflation risks are tilted to the upside, citing potential for more severe and persistent supply issues.
* Participants' views on current economic conditions indicated that the economy had continued to strengthen, driven by progress on vaccinations and strong policy support.

**Mortgage Market:**

* Mortgage originations for home purchases and refinancing remained solid through August.
* Jumbo loan and lower-score Federal Housing Administration borrowers saw improved conditions.

**Consumer Credit:**

* Financing conditions for most consumers with stronger credit scores remained accommodative.
* Consumer credit and credit card market expanded in June, then slowed slightly in July.

**Labor Market:**

* The labor force participation rate edged up, and the employment-to-population ratio rose further.
* Average hourly earnings rose strongly in July and August, reflecting strong labor demand and hiring difficulties.

**Inflation Measures:**

* PCE price index and CPI remained elevated due to surge in demand.

**Key Statistics:**

* Unemployment rate declined to 5.2% in August.
* Labor force participation rate edged up.
* Employment-to-population ratio rose further.
* Average hourly earnings rose strongly in July and August.

**Fed's Economic Outlook:**

* The Committee expects to maintain its accommodative monetary policy stance to support the US economy.
* They agreed that inflation was elevated but expected it to return to target levels over time.
* Members reaffirmed their commitment to achieving maximum employment and price stability goals.

**Tapering Asset Purchases:**

* The Committee discussed tapering asset purchases and interest rate policy.
* They agreed that a gradual tapering process concluding around mid-next year would be appropriate.
* Risk management considerations were key, as participants noted downside risks such as financial condition tightening and upside risks like fiscal expansion or consumer spending increases.

**Conclusion:**

The Federal Reserve will continue to monitor economic progress and adjust its stance as needed. Asset purchases are helping foster smooth market functioning and supporting credit flow. The Committee agreed that a moderation in the pace of asset purchases may soon be warranted if progress continues.

release_time: For release at 2:00 p.m. EDT Share
title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
body:
The Federal Reserve is committed to using its full range of tools to support the U.S. economy in this challenging time, thereby promoting its maximum employment and price stability goals.
With progress on vaccinations and strong policy support, indicators of economic activity and employment have continued to strengthen. The sectors most adversely affected by the pandemic have improved in recent months, but the summer's rise in COVID-19 cases has slowed their recovery. Inflation is elevated, largely reflecting factors that are expected to be transitory. Supply and demand imbalances related to the pandemic and the reopening of the economy have contributed to sizable price increases in some sectors. Overall financial conditions remain accommodative, in part reflecting policy measures to support the economy and the flow of credit to U.S. households and businesses.
The path of the economy continues to depend on the course of the virus. Progress on vaccinations and an easing of supply constraints are expected to support continued gains in economic activity and employment as well as a reduction in inflation. Risks to the economic outlook remain.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. With inflation having run persistently below this longer-run goal, the Committee will aim to achieve inflation moderately above 2 percent for some time so that inflation averages 2 percent over time and longerâterm inflation expectations remain well anchored at 2 percent. The Committee expects to maintain an accommodative stance of monetary policy until these outcomes are achieved. The Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent and expects it will be appropriate to maintain this target range until labor market conditions have reached levels consistent with the Committee's assessments of maximum employment and inflation has risen to 2 percent and is on track to moderately exceed 2 percent for some time. In light of the substantial further progress the economy has made toward the Committee's goals since last December, the Committee decided to begin reducing the monthly pace of its net asset purchases by $10 billion for Treasury securities and $5 billion for agency mortgage-backed securities. Beginning later this month, the Committee will increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $70 billion per month and of agency mortgageâbacked securities by at least $35 billion per month. Beginning in December, the Committee will increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $60 billion per month and of agency mortgage-backed securities by at least $30 billion per month. The Committee judges that similar reductions in the pace of net asset purchases will likely be appropriate each month, but it is prepared to adjust the pace of purchases if warranted by changes in the economic outlook. The Federal Reserve's ongoing purchases and holdings of securities will continue to foster smooth market functioning and accommodative financial conditions, thereby supporting the flow of credit to households and businesses.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Thomas I. Barkin; Raphael W. Bostic; Michelle W. Bowman; Lael Brainard; Richard H. Clarida; Mary C. Daly; Charles L. Evans; Randal K. Quarles; and Christopher J. Waller.

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the multiple chunk summaries in English, within the 10,000-character limit:

**Summary**

The US economy has slowed down due to COVID-19 cases and supply chain issues. Labor market conditions have improved, but inflation remains elevated at 4.4% YoY. The Federal Reserve aims for maximum employment and inflation at 2% over the long run.

**Key Points:**

* Global financial markets saw sovereign yields rise sharply.
* Policy-sensitive rates rose across advanced economies.
* The market-implied path of the federal funds rate rose, implying an earlier date for raising the target range.
* US equity indexes rose, supported by strong earnings reports.
* House prices increased rapidly.

**Key Statistics:**

* 4.4%: YoY inflation rate
* 45 basis points: increase in US five-year measure of inflation compensation based on Treasury Inflation

**Economic Outlook**

The baseline projection for economic activity is skewed to the downside due to concerns about COVID-19 cases and supply issues.

**Key Takeaways**

1. Uncertainty about the economic outlook remains high.
2. Upside risks to inflation include strong demand for goods and a tight labor market.
3. Potential near-term boost to aggregate demand from household savings drawdown.

**FOMC Meeting Minutes**

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) voted unanimously to maintain the federal funds rate and reserve balances at existing levels, with no changes to the target range.

**Conclusions:**

* The economy has shown improvement recently, but COVID-19 cases have slowed recovery.
* Inflation is elevated due to pandemic-related factors, but expected to be transitory.
* The Fed aims for maximum employment and inflation at 2% over the long run.

Overall, the Federal Reserve's monetary policy will focus on achieving maximum employment and inflation at 2% over the long run. The economy faces challenges from COVID-19 cases and supply chain issues, which may impact inflation expectations.

title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
body:
The Federal Reserve is committed to using its full range of tools to support the U.S. economy in this challenging time, thereby promoting its maximum employment and price stability goals.
With progress on vaccinations and strong policy support, indicators of economic activity and employment have continued to strengthen. The sectors most adversely affected by the pandemic have improved in recent months but continue to be affected by COVID-19. Job gains have been solid in recent months, and the unemployment rate has declined substantially. Supply and demand imbalances related to the pandemic and the reopening of the economy have continued to contribute to elevated levels of inflation. Overall financial conditions remain accommodative, in part reflecting policy measures to support the economy and the flow of credit to U.S. households and businesses.
The path of the economy continues to depend on the course of the virus. Progress on vaccinations and an easing of supply constraints are expected to support continued gains in economic activity and employment as well as a reduction in inflation. Risks to the economic outlook remain, including from new variants of the virus.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run. In support of these goals, the Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent. With inflation having exceeded 2 percent for some time, the Committee expects it will be appropriate to maintain this target range until labor market conditions have reached levels consistent with the Committee's assessments of maximum employment. In light of inflation developments and the further improvement in the labor market, the Committee decided to reduce the monthly pace of its net asset purchases by $20 billion for Treasury securities and $10 billion for agency mortgage-backed securities. Beginning in January, the Committee will increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $40 billion per month and of agency mortgageâbacked securities by at least $20 billion per month. The Committee judges that similar reductions in the pace of net asset purchases will likely be appropriate each month, but it is prepared to adjust the pace of purchases if warranted by changes in the economic outlook. The Federal Reserve's ongoing purchases and holdings of securities will continue to foster smooth market functioning and accommodative financial conditions, thereby supporting the flow of credit to households and businesses.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook. The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals. The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H. Powell, Chair; John C. Williams, Vice Chair; Thomas I. Barkin; Raphael W. Bostic; Michelle W. Bowman; Lael Brainard; Richard H. Clarida; Mary C. Daly; Charles L. Evans; Randal K. Quarles; and Christopher J. Waller.

title: Implementation Note issued December 15, 2021
body:
Decisions Regarding Monetary Policy Implementation
The Federal Reserve has made the following decisions to implement the monetary policy stance announced by the Federal Open Market Committee in its statement on December 15, 2021:
- The Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System voted unanimously to maintain the interest rate paid on reserve balances at 0.15 percent, effective December 16, 2021.
The Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System voted unanimously to maintain the interest rate paid on reserve balances at 0.15 percent, effective December 16, 2021.
- As part of its policy decision, the Federal Open Market Committee voted to authorize and direct the Open Market Desk at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, until instructed otherwise, to execute transactions in the System Open Market Account in accordance with the following domestic policy directive: "Effective December 16, 2021, the Federal Open Market Committee directs the Desk to: Undertake open market operations as necessary to maintain the federal funds rate in a target range of 0 to 1/4 percent. Complete the increase in System Open Market Account (SOMA) holdings of Treasury securities by $60 billion and of agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) by $30 billion, as indicated in the monthly purchase plans released in mid- December. Increase the SOMA holdings of Treasury securities by $40 billion and of agency MBS by $20 billion, during the monthly purchase period beginning in mid-January. Increase holdings of Treasury securities and agency MBS by additional amounts as needed to sustain smooth functioning of markets for these securities. Conduct overnight repurchase agreement operations with a minimum bid rate of 0.25 percent and with an aggregate operation limit of $500 billion; the aggregate operation limit can be temporarily increased at the discretion of the Chair. Conduct overnight reverse repurchase agreement operations at an offering rate of 0.05 percent and with a per-counterparty limit of $160 billion per day; the per-counterparty limit can be temporarily increased at the discretion of the Chair. Roll over at auction all principal payments from the Federal Reserve's holdings of Treasury securities and reinvest all principal payments from the Federal Reserve's holdings of agency debt and agency MBS in agency MBS. Allow modest deviations from stated amounts for purchases and reinvestments, if needed for operational reasons. Engage in dollar roll and coupon swap transactions as necessary to facilitate settlement of the Federal Reserve's agency MBS transactions."

title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee
body:
Here is a summary of the text in English, within the 10000-character limit:

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) held its meeting on December 14-15, 2021. Key takeaways include:

* The FOMC reaffirmed its commitment to using all tools to support the US economy during challenging times.
* Participants agreed that inflation has continued to run above 2%, driven by supply and demand imbalances related to the pandemic and economic reopening.
* Labor market progress is rapid, with job gains, declining unemployment rates, and increasing labor force participation.
* The Committee aims to keep the federal funds rate at 0-1/4% until labor markets reach maximum employment.

Notable numbers include:

* $20 billion (reduction in Treasury security purchases)
* $10 billion (reduction in agency MBS purchases)

The Committee also discussed:

* The importance of considering differences between the current normalization episode and previous ones.
* The potential for a faster pace of policy rate normalization due to a stronger economic outlook, higher inflation, and a tighter labor market.
* The decision to initiate balance sheet runoff will be data-dependent.

Overall, the FOMC is committed to supporting the US economy through monetary policy tools while maintaining flexibility to adjust policy based on evolving economic conditions.
